data.title: Bitfarms: Capitalizing On The Next Generation Of AI Data Centers
data.description:
Bitfarms (BITF) (BITF:CA) is a company that will transform its cryptocurrency mining farms into data centers for intensive use of HPC and AI. The expectation is to generate strong enough revenue to replace cryptocurrencies by 2027.

What I'm seeing in this very recent process is that the company is already building a strategy roadmap to face big energy demands of the future. Above all else, I'm seeing a bottleneck between chips and data centers that could boost long-term revenue

Cryptocurrency still represents the major part of the company's income (over 90%), but the plan has already been described. The volatility of cryptocurrency prices led the company to change its business model. From cryptocurrencies to HPC and AI data centers. From volatile income to fixed lease agreements, Bitfarms aims to lease its plants at the highest possible price.

Of course, that's something any owner would do when renting their plant, as data center REITs like Equinix (EQIX) or Digital Realty (DLR), to name a few, do. But in the case of gigawatts, the company's forecast is a 45-gigawatt deficit for 2030 (see image below). This will cause lease contracts to become (and it's already happening) increasingly expensive. Now they are at a peak of $150 per kilowatt per month, while we can take as reference the $120 agreed in 2024 between Core Scientific and CoreWeave.

If we look at history, lease rates for the past 20 years were 3% annually, but now, with the AI ​​boom and large investments in data centers, the rate is projected to reach 12% annually from 2022 onwards. These data can be seen in the image below, which Gagnon titled "Infrastructure is not a bubble" referring to the fact that modern computing has boosted data center capacity at a rate of 8.8% annually over the past 20 years.

Modern computing as a motor of the data center was crushed with a brake until 2021, as you can see in the chart, until AI fueled the rise again. This is a revolution we are already seeing. As Gagnon said in the conference call, while data centers were previously measured in kilowatts, in less than 3 years, AI has led to data centers now being measured in megawatts and gigawatts. Another example he mentioned was racks, which used to support 10 kilowatts, and now they can support 370 kilowatts.

In other words, the demand grew by leaps and bounds in a very short time. The question Gagnon asks (and so do I) is, do we have the infrastructure and the energy to cover that demand? The answer is no, and they won't have it for a long time. Let's see this in numbers. According to a Coface study, for every $1 billion invested in data centers, $125 million of investment in energy will be needed. If I take the 2025 investment in data center equipment as $475 billion, that means an additional investment of $56.25 billion would be needed. Projections show even more growth in future investments.

Globally, more than $750 billion worth of projects could be delayed by 2030 because of these bottlenecks.

Coface Report

So, what I'm seeing is that all this will cause an inevitable bottleneck, where NVIDIA (NVDA) silicon chips and other manufacturers will keep on flooding the market, but the infrastructure and energy needed to make it operational are still lacking. For example, a complete network connection to supply an intensive data center can take 5 years, according to Coface. So, how can Bitfarms play a key role in this environment? That's what I'll try to explain.

But before, I'd like to make clear that this introduction will be better explained by Gagnon in the conference call, which I recommend reading if you have some free time. I must admit it convinced me, but of course, I still can't rule out risks along that path, which we'll see later.

Company Overview: The Potential of the Bitfarms portfolio

When I first observed the company's internal efforts to face the bottleneck mentioned, I found an interesting point: the need for cooling in AI-intensive data centers. This isn't something new, but I really like the way the press addressed it. This isn't something minor because if the structural conditions are better for cooling, there will be cost savings in PuE.

The best example is the State of Texas, a natural area for the installation of data centers given the facilities for power generation. Texas is the state with the most data centers in the country, behind Virginia. However, the company's policy is to propose an alternative to Texas due to its climatic and geographical disadvantages. Let's read what the CEO himself says:

Building and operating data centers in a hot, arid desert climate like Texas as opposed to cooler northern climates like Pennsylvania, Washington and Quebec means more CapEx and OpEx for cooling. This isn't an opinion. It's math and engineering. If we built our exact same data center for Panther Creek with the same design, equipment and materials in Texas, it would have a PuE of about 1.4 to about 1.5. Whereas in Pennsylvania, Quebec or Washington, it would be about 1.2 to 1.3. That means for every megawatt we are converting, more of those electrons are going to compute, which is the revenue-generating activity for customers as opposed to supporting revenue generation through cooling.

Based on this positioning strategy, Pennsylvania and Quebec are the foundation of Bitfarms' potential. Of its 13 cryptocurrency mining farms (the future data centers), 8 are in Quebec, Canada, 4 are in the US, and 1 is in Paraguay. The company's goal is to get out of the Latin American market, so its farm Paso Pe in Paraguay is in the process of being sold in the short term. The goal is to focus on the US, which makes sense given the technological potential of the country. In fact, the three largest energy power facilities are located in Pennsylvania, in the US.

Panther Creek is one of those 3 key campuses in eastern Pennsylvania, with a total capacity of 470 MW: 410 are guaranteed to operate with established contracts. Panther Creek will try to surpass the 500 MW in the future with more infrastructure starting in Q4 2025, while they are also planning for it to be a key place for the operations of Nvidia's Vera Rubin GPU chips. Vera Rubin is the next generation of Nvidia chips after Blackwell, with an estimated distribution from the end of 2026.

The other data center that will be prepared for the service of Vera Rubin GPU is Sharon, with a total operating capacity guaranteed by contract of 110 MW, but which would only be fully operational by the end of 2026, as would Panther Creek. Meanwhile, Sharon is working with 30 MW for Bitcoin mining.

Meanwhile, the 8 sites in Quebec also play an important role with 170 MW of total capacity and operating at full capacity, all located 90 minutes from Montreal. I'm seeing in Quebec the continuity of a secure cooling strategy because this place has cool weather and one of the lowest energy costs in North America. Plans for expansion and conversion to HPC and AI are still in their initial stages, but the strategy is focused on Sherbrooke. Anyway, being close to the 8 locations, I believe, could give a guarantee of a more secure and interconnected service for hyperscalers.

Bitfarms' other gem is Scrubgrass, also in Pennsylvania, which currently operates at 60 MW but with an estimated potential capacity increase of 1,300 MW (1.3 GW), which would be the biggest quantitative leap in energy supply for the company. It would also be the first data center for HPC and AI outside Texas, with more than 1 GW of power. However, this development still needs to guarantee the power source, which could be partially resolved with the fiber optic networks in the vicinity of Pittsburgh and Cleveland.

But fiber optic networks would not be enough. A more viable alternative would be the building of a 4-mile pipeline from Scrubgrass to Tennessee, which could supply 550 megawatts of natural gas. But because this engineering isn't over yet, the estimated operation of the more ambitious data centers of Bitfarms is calculated for 2028.

In summary, I'm not seeing a premium data center player, but I am seeing a very intelligent strategy with solid foundations about a bottleneck between chips and energetic infrastructure and an expansion potential of 2,111 MW. This level of energy power for data centers would put Bitfarms among the major players of the sector, especially with the Scrubgrass boost.

Q3 2025: An Extraordinary Revaluation

Beyond future projections regarding the conversion to data centers, the company continues to generate revenue largely through cryptocurrency mining, which brought in $60.4 million in the quarter, a 136% YoY increase. Given that total revenue was $69.2 million, cryptocurrency mining accounts for approximately 87% of revenue.

However, the revenue increase is not accompanied by a margin increase. The gross mining profit was 35%, 120 BPS less than the previous quarter and 900 bps YoY. And this is something I'm also seeing in the increases in sales costs, which grew 148% YoY, above the 136% YoY of the revenues. Next you'll see two pictures: the breakdown by revenue and its costs for operations.

Anyway, the company compensates for this cut in the margins with an Adj EBITDA relatively stable during the quarters, between $8 and $9 million. In Q3 there was an extraordinary revaluation in a gain from warrants that at the same time raised the Adj EBITDA to $19 million, but the normal thing would be to see this figure returning to its average in the next quarters.

The net loss for continuing operations was $48 million ($0.08 per share), a considerable increase compared to the $23 million loss of Q3 2024. I think we must see the losses in the context of the thesis planned by the company, based on the generation of future income.

My intention isn't to look at the losses but the way the expansion projects of their plants and the cash flow will be financed. That's why I focused, first, on the reserve fund of over $1 billion in cash and bitcoin, as explained by CFO Jonathan Mir, which seemed like a solid sum to me. And second, the total cash of the company is $86 million with a total debt of $73 million.

This gives me some security that the company doesn't depend on a high indebtedness and can continue raising capital. Anyway, there is still a debt margin, and I think it will moderately grow in the short term. For example, Bitfarms issued $588 million in senior convertible notes in October at an annual rate of 1.37%, maturing in 2031, with a conversion price of $6.86 per share starting in 2030.

What I want to highlight is that I'm not seeing an inability to finance because this sector is characterized by constantly attracting capital for future investments given the boom in AI data centers.

Valuation: How Will It Impact Profitability on Share Price

To better understand the intrinsic value of the company and how future profitability could make an impact on the share price, I have built a DCF model to evaluate the upside projection, considering that we might not see a positive EBIT until 2027.

I chose to start with an EBIT loss of $88 million, taking as a reference the operating income for the 9 months of 2025: $56.8 million. In 2027, with the implementation of the data center conversion and based on the company's estimate, I raised EBIT to $267.7 million. I'm basing my estimation on the Market Screener site, which would lead to an EBIT margin near 40%. I consider this estimation very precise because the last time Bitfarms had a positive EBIT, in 2021, the EBIT margin was also near 40% (Financials, Seeking Alpha). Finally, with the consolidation of its business model (including the Scrubgrass operation) and the high prices of lease contracts, I'm ending my model at $555.2 million.

Year 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029
Revenue $276.6 $265.5 $661.5 $991.5 $1,388.2
Change 43% -4% 149% 50% 40%
EBIT Margin -32% -29% 40% 40% 40%
EBIT $88 $77.5 $267.7 $396.4 $555.2

So, because the company could play a key role in the next AI data center generation, I'm applying a growth rate in the long term of 3.5% due to the large investments in the sector and the mentioned bottleneck. For WACC, I took the information from the site called GuruFocus, which gives 9.2%, and for the tax rate, I'm applying an assumption of 22%. I got this rate from the 2024 tax expense calculation, but since the company is still losing money, let's take this as an assumption.

Tax rate 22%
Share price $3.10
Shares outstanding 597M

Given that the share price is very volatile, I think it will stay this way until the consolidation of the data center conversion. This consolidation, and based on my estimated DCF model of business success, the upside is 100%.

Risks

The company faces some risks, mostly those related to the execution of its plan within the established time, because if they stretch a lot, it will be more difficult to achieve the estimated income. In other words, these risks are efficiency and the conversion of cryptocurrency mining to data centers.

Another risk I must mention is the business strategy based on the bottleneck between chips and infrastructure. If the estimated bottleneck raises the price before Bitfarms achieves the conversion, the peak prices per kilowatt might not last too long. We must take into account that AI's race in hyperscalers goes side by side with the optimal infrastructure's race of data centers.

Finally, the feelings of the stock market along with the country's macroeconomy will be two factors to look closely at. In the first case, AI has led to huge increases on Wall Street amid big investment ads, and the fears of a major correction in high valuations remain a risk. On the other hand, the US economy and its development in terms of activity and employment will play a key role, for example, in the Fed's monetary policy. Although it's true we are going into a lower interest rate environment, cycles can change abruptly, and startups and companies that are not yet profitable may be more vulnerable.

Conclusions

Bitfarms integrates a set of companies that, because of their energetic capacity for cryptocurrency mining, can now be part of the race for data centers of AI. I believe Bitfarms stands out because of its smart investment plan (which doesn't even come close to the amounts of money that hyperscalers are spending) and a map of more than a dozen plants strategically located to have the lowest cost and to achieve the most expensive possible lease agreements

In this context, if Bitfarms fulfills its current plans of power capacity (it could reach 2 GW in the next 2-3 years), its share price could be triggered, but not in the moment the income is generated, but before, when the market detects this potential profitability and rewards the company's valuation. I believe we are seeing one of the best buying options in the market of future data centers. My recommendation is to buy at the current prices.

data.title: Sabesp: Good Potential In A Conservative Industry
data.description:
Thesis

As I mentioned in the introduction, the Companhia de Saneamento Básico do Estado de São Paulo (SBS) (hereinafter SABESP) is dedicated to water treatment for distribution to end consumers, as well as providing sewage services in the state of São Paulo, Brazil. Through its operations in the 375 municipalities that comprise the state, the company provides treated water services to more than 9.5 million connection points and 8.2 million sewage connections.

As can be seen below, the company operates in one of Brazil's most prosperous states. While the national GDP per capita is around BRL 42,200 ($7,900), in São Paulo it is around BRL 58,300 ($10,925). Similarly, while drinking water coverage in Brazil is around 84.9% and wastewater coverage is around 56%, in the state of São Paulo the percentages are much higher. In the state, these percentages are 95.2% and 90.5%, respectively.

Before analyzing the company's growth, I think it's important to clarify that the water utilities industry, due to its business structure, is typically an industry whose growth is linked to population growth. Since these companies hold a natural monopoly due to heavy investment in network infrastructure, which limits competition, an increase in the number of users cannot be generated solely through an increase in market share.

That said, two areas where SABESP has shown very strong performance can be identified. First, the service fee increase since Q1 2024 largely driven by the rise in the Brazilian FX exchange rate. Second, a considerable increase in revenue from large users. As the company explains, this is due to a reduction in discounts for this type of user and a smaller portion related to retroactive collections for precautionary measures that SABESP successfully obtained.

Now, if we analyze another key point I'd like to highlight, it's the increase in treated water production, as well as the number of connections. In that regard, we can see that since Q3 2024, the company has made a significant leap, going from an average of 775 billion cubic meters to the current figure of over 808 billion. This represents a 4.4% increase for the year, which, from my perspective, is a noteworthy increase for this type of company.

Regarding water connections, an increase was also observed in the last year, rising from 9.45 million to 9.5 million. Even so, the percentage increase is considerably lower, accumulating a 0.6% increase for the year. Nevertheless, as mentioned, in a state like São Paulo, with its level of economic development compared to the rest of Brazil and with a well-established company, the fact that connections can increase as a result of urban expansion is noteworthy.

Another point I'd like to mention concerns SABESP's negotiations with Brazilian authorities to move forward with the acquisition of EMAE (Empresa Metropolitana de Aguas e Energía S.A.). This company, which primarily focuses on hydroelectric power generation and water resource management in the São Paulo area, could bring about a vertical expansion in SABESP's business model.

The company has a production potential of 961 MW with stable revenues tied to inflation, similar to the case of SABESP, according to the M&A presentation (page 4). Following this potential acquisition, SBS expects a significant increase in the company's managed reservoirs for human consumption. This, in turn, leads to greater integration of the Billings-Pinheiros Tietê water system.

In this regard, the company reported in the last conference call that they expect to be able to close the deal by Q4 of this year or, at the latest, Q1 2026:

Lastly, a brief update on the EMAE acquisition. Following the signing, all regulatory documentation was filed with CADE and ANEEL in early October. We now move into the approval phase, consistent with transactions of this nature and scale. Subject to regulatory approvals, closing is expected between late Q4 and early Q1 of next year with payment occurring only upon completion. This transaction reinforces long-term water security and energy efficiency, further integrating SABESP's strategic infrastructure.

Finally, to conclude, I would like to briefly mention the debt structure for the coming years. SABESP's debt amortization outlook is quite favorable for the next four years, as shown in the table below. As can be seen, the company has sufficient cash on hand to meet its debt amortization obligations over the next four years. In fact, as you will see in the valuation section, SBS's debt-to-EBITDA ratio is lower than that of leading water utilities in other regions.

Taking this into account, it is a very interesting advantage that the company, in addition to maintaining more or less predictable cash flows, is in an unleveraged position that gives it a margin of safety.

Given the potential of the deal, coupled with a relatively unleveraged capital structure and growth that, while modest, must be understood in the context of a consolidated and developed industry with relatively stable cash flows over time, I consider SABESP a good BUY opportunity.

3Q2025 Results

In this section, I'd like to briefly summarize the company's results for the last quarter and compare them to previous periods. To do this, I'd like to present not only the numbers themselves but also an analysis that explains in more detail the factors behind the differences between periods.

To begin, I'd like to mention that at the close of the results period, the FX exchange rate was BRL 5.58 = $1. That said, I think it's important to mention the figures reported by SABESP and then move on to the adjusted results. In that regard, the first thing I'd like to highlight is the revenue the company reported for Q3 2025. During this quarter, SABESP reported a value of BRL 9.425 billion, which represents a significant drop compared to the nearly BRL 15 billion reported in Q3 2024. However, when we delve deeper into the results report, what stands out is a decline in the Financial Assets segment, which fell from BRL 8.8 billion in Q3 2024 to BRL 154 million.

Regarding EBITDA, the reported value for Q3 2025 was BRL 3,069 million. Looking at the same quarter of 2024, the company reported an EBITDA of BRL 10,450 million. This difference aligns with the previously mentioned Financial Assets figure. These figures represent a 70% year-over-year decrease, which is extremely concerning from this perspective alone.

Now let's analyze the adjusted figures. Looking at revenues, the adjusted figure for the third quarter of 2025 was BRL 5,500 million (approximately $985.66 million). Compared to the adjusted figure for the same quarter in 2024, we see a slight increase from BRL 5,470 million. Regarding EBITDA, the adjusted figure is around BRL 3,069 million (equivalent to $550 million), while in Q3 2024 it was BRL 3,200 million. Annualizing the last quarter's figure and comparing it to the company's EV ($22.83 B), we see that the EV/EBITDA ratio is around 10.19x. While this is higher than the figures for the companies I usually cover, it seems low to me for an industry like water utilities, in which SABESP operates.

The last point I'd like to highlight is net cash from operating activities. During 3Q2025, the company generated BRL 3,173 million ($568.65 million). Annualizing this figure and comparing it to the company's market capitalization, we see that the P/CF ratio is around 7.95x. Again, for a company with this rather predictable business model, a figure of this magnitude is, in my opinion, quite attractive.

Valuation

In this section, I intend to present a valuation of the company. Given the type of industry in which SBS operates, with a public that depends on the provision of drinking water and a near-monopoly, I believe the best approach is to perform a valuation using multiples compared to other companies in the industry.

To perform this valuation, I used the list of comparable companies from the Peers section of Seeking Alpha. While these companies are based in different countries, primarily the United States, I believe that despite the differences between countries, their business models are structurally similar. Therefore, my criterion was to find companies with a similar market capitalization. Once the comparable companies were listed, I divided the analysis into two parts. First, a valuation segment based on multiples. Second, a list of some of the economic and financial variables that I consider relevant to understanding SBS's position compared to the other selected companies in the industry.

Starting with the first segment, the first thing I want to mention is the ratios I decided to use to value the company. In that regard, I consider the most representative for characterizing the industry to be the P/E ratio, the EV/EBITDA ratio, and the P/CF ratio. For each of the selected multiples, a unique ratio was calculated for the companies based on a market capitalization-weighted average. Once the three values ​​were calculated, I obtained a target price for each. Then, using the weighting factors shown in the table below, I averaged the values ​​to arrive at a single target price. As you can see, I decided to give more weight to the EV/EBITDA ratio since companies in this industry tend to have a highly leveraged capital structure, so I believe this ratio reflects the industry structure more efficiently than the other two.

Based on the results obtained, it can be seen that SBS has an upside potential, which, in my view, for a company in the water utilities industry, which is generally a mature industry with rather conservative characteristics, is a potential that is extremely interesting.

Now I would like to move on to the second point, in which I set out to analyze SBS's relative position compared to the other selected companies. To do this, I decided to analyze three of the companies' financial and economic indicators and see how SBS ranks in relation to them. Specifically, I used EBITDA Margin, EBITDA CAGR, and Net Debt to EBITDA as indicators. Considering the latter two, SBS appears to be considerably higher than the average of the other companies. Only in the case of EBITDA Margin does the Brazilian company fall below the average of the selected companies.

Based on the upside potential and this better performance of SBS compared to other companies, it can be seen that this valuation adds greater robustness to the investment thesis described previously in which I indicate that I consider SBS as a BUY.

Risks

Finally, I would like to mention the short-term risks, which are primarily linked to the Brazilian economy. Specifically, if the downward trend in the FX exchange rate continues and this impacts inflation, SBS's revenues could be eroded. While it is true that some fees are adjusted for inflation, the mechanism is not automatic, and this could lead to lower margins.

On the other hand, the possibility that government authorities may object to EMAE's M&A process could lead to some market pessimism regarding the company's vertical growth potential. In this sense, observing how the deal unfolds could be important as a catalyst, depending on whether it is approved or not.

data.title: Tradeweb Government Bond Update - November 2025
data.description:
In a reversal of October’s trend, global 10-year government bond yields increased in November, except those for France and the U.S., which fell by two and eight basis points, respectively.

Japan’s 10-year government bond yield reached a 17-year high of 1.84% on November 20 before ending the month slightly lower at 1.81%. The country’s annual inflation rate edged up to 3% in October from 2.9% in September, the highest reading since July this year.

Taichi Shibuya, Head of Japan at Tradeweb, said: “Japanese government bond yields continued to push higher in November. Those on 10-year debt rose 16 basis points during the month, as investors reassessed the Bank of Japan’s policy path and the government’s fiscal policy. While yields eased slightly into month-end, the upward move underscores the market’s sensitivity to shifts in policy expectations and broader global rate dynamics.”

However, the month’s biggest mover was the yield on the Australian 10-year bond, which climbed nearly 22 basis points to 4.51%. On November 4, the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) kept its cash rate unchanged at 3.6%, maintaining borrowing costs at their lowest level since April 2023. The country’s annual inflation rose to 3.8% in October from 3.6% in the prior month, remaining above the RBA’s 2–3% target.

In the U.S., the yield on the 10-year Treasury benchmark note closed the month at 4.02%. The prolonged government shutdown meant that few major economic indicators were released, leaving a noticeable gap in the month’s data flow and fewer signals for investors to gauge the economy’s near-term trajectory.

Over in Europe, the UK 10-year Gilt yield increased just four basis points during the month to 4.45%. The country’s annual inflation rate eased to 3.6% in October, the lowest level in four months, while the S&P Global UK Manufacturing PMI rose to 50.2 in November from 49.7 in the prior month, pointing to a modest improvement in activity.

In contrast, the GfK Consumer Confidence Index fell to -19 in November from -17, as households braced for Chancellor Rachel Reeves’ Autumn Budget, which included freezes to income-tax thresholds and new levies on property, dividends and electric vehicles, alongside higher welfare spending and public investment.

In Germany, the 10-year Bund yield climbed five basis points to 2.69% at month-end. According to preliminary estimates, the country’s annual inflation rate remained unchanged at 2.3% in November. Meanwhile, the HCOB Germany Manufacturing PMI dropped to 48.2 from 49.6 in October, amid ongoing customer uncertainty and weaker overseas demand.

In neighbouring France, political turbulence persisted through November as the budget standoff deepened. The government under Sébastien Lecornu, himself only recently reappointed as prime minister, put pressure on lawmakers to pass the 2026 budget by year-end, after the lower house rejected the tax side of the legislation.

The French 10-year government bond yield ended the month at 3.41%, and its annual inflation rate held steady at 0.9%, unchanged from October and below the 1% forecast, according to preliminary estimates.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Global ETF Equity Outlook: Here's Where Market Leadership Could Shift In 2026 (Hint, Likely Not The U.S.)
data.description:
By Dina Ting, CFA, Head of Global Index Portfolio Management, Franklin Templeton ETFs

Global markets are entering 2026 with widening dispersion, lowering cross-country correlations and a shifting interest rate landscape that is reshaping relative equity opportunities. After several years dominated by a narrow group of large-capitalization US names, investors now face a more varied, region-driven market. With policy cycles, earnings paths and structural growth drivers pulling in different directions, we believe broad global diversification—with targeted country tilts—may be key to capturing the next wave of leadership.

Regardless of whether artificial intelligence (AI) enthusiasm proves overdone, the broader US economy is clearly slowing. Sentiment weakened heading into the "Black Friday" sales season, and all three components of The Conference Board’s Expectations Index—business conditions, job prospects and future income—fell in November. As the organization’s chief economist noted, “Mid-2026 expectations for labor market conditions remained decidedly negative, and expectations for increased household incomes shrunk dramatically after six months of strongly positive readings.”

What’s more, many investors continue to have limited exposure to international markets within their portfolios. Single-country exchange-traded funds (ETFs) can help broaden global allocations and add diversification by accessing markets with unique long-term growth characteristics. While the Federal Reserve is easing cautiously, parts of Europe appear closer to stabilizing, with pockets of above-trend momentum emerging. Diverging rate paths are reinforcing this global split. In the United Kingdom, we expect steady Bank of England cuts to relieve consumer pressure while boosting the appeal of high-dividend stocks.

Across Asia, several central banks remain in easing mode. If US growth cools while Asian momentum holds, market leadership could broaden further. In South Korea, even incremental Bank of Korea cuts could lift exporters and tech firms by improving funding conditions and helping fuel the global semiconductor rebound. Meanwhile, some economists expect Brazil’s central bank to trim its current elevated rates, lowering financing costs across banks and consumer sectors. Mexico’s Banxico has already begun easing and may continue if inflation stays contained—supporting both corporate activity and household demand.

Together, we believe these shifts point to a more supportive monetary backdrop in 2026 for investors ready to look beyond the United States.

Recent correlation trends also indicate that markets such as Taiwan, Japan and South Korea have seen their correlations with the S&P 500 Index decline over the past year.

Global Markets’ Correlation with S&P 500 (Last One Year)

As of October 31, 2025

Global Markets’ Correlation with S&P 500 (Last Three Years)

As of October 31, 2025

Falling cross-country correlations amplify diversification benefits

Diverging policy paths, currencies and sector exposures are producing more idiosyncratic returns, allowing international allocations to contribute more meaningfully to portfolio resilience.

The United Kingdom offers compelling value, in our analysis. Sticky but moderating inflation and ongoing Bank of England rate cuts support its defensive, income-heavy market. UK-US equity correlation has dropped 57%, falling from roughly 0.30 over three years through October 31, 2025, to 0.13 over one year through the same date—a meaningful shift that enhances the United Kingdom’s diversification role within global portfolios.1

We believe Brazil is positioned as a value and income opportunity supported by commodities, interest rate cuts and fiscal discipline. Government forecasts now call for gross domestic product growth of roughly 2.4% in 2026, with inflation easing toward the country’s official 3% target.2 Valuations remain attractive to us relative to emerging‑market peers. If global manufacturing and commodity cycles reaccelerate alongside domestic monetary easing, then Brazil could continue delivering late‑cycle cyclicality and income.

Taiwan remains central to the AI and semiconductor cycle. Global chip sales, following a stronger-than-expected third quarter, are projected to grow more than 25%, with the World Semiconductor Trade Statistics (WSTS) forecasting the market may approach US$1 trillion in 2026.3 Taiwan should continue to benefit disproportionately from this trend, but we believe valuations have become richer. And while the AI capital-expenditure cycle continues and export demand remains resilient, geopolitical risks remain acute.

Notwithstanding these pressures, we believe Taiwan may continue to provide growth upside with moderate cross‑market correlation. Its equity correlation with the S&P 500 now stands at roughly -0.26 on a one-year basis—the lowest level among the major global markets shown.4 This places Taiwan well below other large economies such as the United Kingdom, Germany, India and China, all of which maintain positive correlations with the US market. Even compared with other export-driven Asian markets, Taiwan’s decoupling is notable: South Korea’s correlation, while also lower this year, remains meaningfully higher than Taiwan’s. We believe this sharp divergence underscores the island’s increasingly distinctive return profile and its growing value as a diversifying allocation within global portfolios.

The new year for Japan has a fresh stimulus package to look forward to under the leadership of recently appointed Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, whose cabinet has approved US$117 billion in stimulus focused on energy security, defense modernization, infrastructure upgrades and household support.5 Combined with corporate-governance reforms and a potential stabilization in the Japanese yen, these priorities could provide ongoing support for value-oriented sectors, industrials and domestically focused companies.

India, meanwhile, continues to attract global attention as investors weigh whether the long-anticipated acceleration in its equity markets will finally materialize. After a year of elevated expectations—due to resilient domestic demand, strong earnings and enthusiasm around manufacturing reforms—the question heading into 2026 is whether those structural drivers can translate into sustained market leadership. Recent policy moves, including India’s approval of a rare-earth, permanent-magnet manufacturing program, reinforce the government’s commitment to deepening industrial capacity and reducing import dependence. If execution remains strong and inflation stays contained, India may be better positioned to deliver the breakout performance that investors have been waiting for.

As 2026 unfolds, we believe falling correlations and diverging policy regimes argue for broad global exposure—while selective tilts toward markets with clear structural or policy catalysts, such as Japan and India, may help capture more resilient performance.

Endnotes

  2. There is no assurance that any estimate, forecast or projection will be realized.

  3. Source: “Global Semiconductor Market Approaches $1T in 2026.” WSTS. December 2, 2025.

  5. Source: “Japan to Add Short-Term Debt to Fund Takaichi’s Package.” Bloomberg. November 28, 2025. There is no assurance that any estimate, forecast or projection will be realized.

What are the risks?

Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Large-capitalization companies may fall out of favor with investors based on market and economic conditions.

ETFs trade like stocks, fluctuate in market value and may trade above or below the ETF’s net asset value. Brokerage commissions and ETF expenses will reduce returns. ETF shares may be bought or sold throughout the day at their market price on the exchange on which they are listed. However, there can be no guarantee that an active trading market for ETF shares will be developed or maintained or that their listing will continue or remain unchanged. While the shares of ETFs are tradable on secondary markets, they may not readily trade in all market conditions and may trade at significant discounts in periods of market stress.

International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Investments in companies in a specific country or region may experience greater volatility than those that are more broadly diversified geographically. The government’s participation in the economy is still high and, therefore, investments in China will be subject to larger regulatory risk levels compared to many other countries.

data.title: GeoPark: High Potential, But Still A Long Way To Go After Its Landing In Vaca Muerta
data.description:
Junior oil producer, GeoPark (GPRK), recently announced its landing in Argentina's Vaca Muerta shale oil region, one of the most promising in the world for its shale oil reserves. With the acquisition of two significant blocks in the Neuquén Basin, GPRK has kick-started a transformational shift in its business. The Bogotá-based firm will move from exclusively operating mature conventional oil fields to greenfield projects. Furthermore, it will concentrate its operations primarily in Colombia and Argentina, after selling assets in Ecuador and Brazil this year.

I must say that I've been wanting to write about this company for some time, and some readers had asked me to start covering it. However, I decided to wait until the company's entry into the Neuquén Basin was finalized to have a clearer picture of its prospects.

While the company anticipates a significant capex investment in the coming years to accelerate its developments in Vaca Muerta, it needs to work extensively on its cost management, as we will see below. While the arrival of one of the most promising shale oil basins on the planet presents significant challenges, starting with its cost structure, given that it is a relatively small operator still inexperienced in this type of formation, and as it enters an increasingly competitive market dominated by well-established players with access to financing.

Therefore, and due to current execution risks, I am assigning a Hold rating in this initial analysis, pending further developments in Vaca Muerta. That said, I maintain a predominantly constructive view on GPRK, after the stock rebounded in the last month, approaching a bearish resistance level around $10.

The Vaca Muerta Bet

But let me briefly introduce you to some technical aspects of the acquisition. After the failed attempt to acquire a percentage of Phoenix Global's operations in the region, GPRK managed to acquire 100% of Loma Jarillosa Este and Puesto Silva Oeste, two blocks located in one of the most productive areas of the field, for $115M. This gives the company greater control over the assets, although the risks are also greater. During the presentation at the last investor day, GPRK announced its intention to allocate between $290M and $320M in capex per year until 2028, while setting a target of reaching almost 50kboe/d by 2030, with adjusted EBITDA of between $520M and $550M, including operations in Argentina.

Furthermore, and as expected, with the addition of the new assets, GPRK's 2P (proven and probable) reserves grew by 38% year-over-year to 121.3 Mboe, according to the company's latest inventory report. Of these, approximately 36.7 Mboe correspond to the two new blocks in Argentina, which now represent around 30% of total reserves, thus diluting Colombia's share and achieving healthy diversification.

Q3 2025 Results

Financial Results

Turning now to the company's latest quarterly figures, we find generally quite solid results considering the current international oil price environment. Financially, it's worth noting that expectations were quite low after GPRK posted its lowest revenue level since Q4 2020 ($120M) in Q2. That said, revenue for this last quarter was $125M, beating analysts consensus of $116.5M by 7%. This was partly due to increased production and somewhat more stable prices after a turbulent first half of the year, marked by geopolitical tensions. Moreover, a rationalization process undertaken by the company, including the sale of some operations in Colombia and Brazil, managed to keep costs under control during Q3.

At the bottom line, Q3 net earnings came in at $15.9M, or $0.31 per share, in line with the consensus forecast and reversing a loss posted of $0.2 p/sh in Q2. It is worth mentioning that excluding an extraordinary $7.5M exploration write-off charge registered in the Putumayo Basin, adjusted net income would have reached $23.4M ($0.45 p/sh), which underscores its quarterly robust performance even more. In turn, at a profitability level, adjusted EBITDA totaled $71.4M at a 57% margin, consistent with Q2 levels but still remaining below the 63% margin achieved in the prior year.

Operating Results

On the operational front, consolidated average production reached 27.1Kboe/d during Q3 (+3% Q/Q), driven primarily by a rebound in the company's two main operations in Colombia (Llanos 34 and CPO-5). Meanwhile, the average operating cost of $12.5 per barrel came in line with management's annual expectations and showed a 13% Y/Y reduction, which places GPRK at very competitive levels for the Colombian industry, although insufficient for the Vaca Muerta market, as we will see next.

If we look at the local market, competitors such as Ecopetrol (EC) reported lifting costs of $12.2 per barrel in Q3 despite operating at a massively larger scale (752Kboe/d versus GPRK's 28Kboe/d), which is quite good actually. In turn, we have to bear in mind that other factors also work in favor of the Colombian state oil company, such as its dominant position in the upstream/downstream local market, which allows it to negotiate better prices and obtain higher margins.

However, when compared to competitors in Vaca Muerta, the cost gap widens significantly. YPF (YPF), Argentina's leading operator in the play, reported average lifting costs of approximately $9 per barrel in its Vaca Muerta operations for Q3 and aims to further reduce them to $5 per barrel. Meanwhile, other competitors, such as Vista Energy (VIST), the primary independent producer in Vaca Muerta, maintain operating costs around $11 per barrel, with plans for additional reductions as production scales in the coming years to supply a growing export market in Argentina (as I described in my latest VIST coverage).

From my perspective, this presents one of GPRK's most significant challenges in the coming years. If GPRK attempts to operate in Vaca Muerta with a cost structure similar to its Colombian operations, it could face a competitive cost disadvantage of around 30% relative to YPF and 12% compared to VIST at these levels. This is compounded by its lack of experience in unconventional shale oil production (prevalent in the Argentine formation), raising concerns that investors must continue to monitor.

Debt and Cash Flow

On the balance sheet front, the company ended the quarter with $197M in cash, down from $277M a year earlier, reflecting in part disbursements for the Vaca Muerta assets acquisition. The firm received approximately $16M from the sale of the Llanos 32 block in Colombia and the Manati gas field in Brazil, as management redirects its focus toward developing operations in Argentina's premier oil field.

Operating cash flow posted a year-to-date deficit of $40M, though this represented improvement on a quarterly basis from the -$85.8M cumulative position at the end of June, driven by earnings recovery in the latest quarter. GPRK issued $550M in 2030 notes earlier this year at a considerably higher cost than the 2027 issuance (8.75% versus 5.5%), but successfully extended its maturity profile from 2 to 5 years. Notably, the offering attracted over $1.1B in demand, underscoring institutional appetite for the company's credit. In the most recent quarter, the company also repurchased $108M of these notes, which will generate $9.5M in annual interest savings.

GPRK's leverage levels relative to EBITDA have increased in recent quarters, though they remain comfortably manageable. Perhaps most notably, the interest coverage ratio (while still at healthy levels) has shown a significant decline over the past year amid weakening results, which adds another source of concern for investors going forward.

However, net debt remains virtually unchanged compared to a year ago, still providing flexibility should the company require additional financing for future Vaca Muerta development. Meanwhile, the 2030 Notes include covenants that, among other requirements, stipulate Net Debt to Adjusted EBITDA must not exceed 3.5 times, while interest coverage must be maintained above 2.5 times for GeoPark to incur new debt.

Valuation

At current levels around $8, GPRK shares reflect market caution regarding near-term risks, including Colombia's production decline and Argentina's execution challenges. The stock experienced a significant rally in late October following Argentina's midterm election results but has since remained quite stable.

My base case projects the shares could reach $9.50-$10.00 in the coming months, testing a longer-term resistance level in that zone, as shares with exposure to Argentina have regained momentum since last October's elections in the country and the company posted a solid quarter. However, meaningful upside ($12-$15) would require concrete evidence of progress in Vaca Muerta. This will ultimately depend on the evolution of operating permits during Q1 2026 and results from the first wells during 2H 2026. That said, an additional catalyst could be Colombia's upcoming presidential elections, which may signal a political shift in government, although I think it is still a little early for the electoral 'trade.'

In valuation terms, GPRK currently trades at a meaningful discount to the industry average, which may offer an entry point for patient investors with a longer-term horizon and higher risk tolerance. However, this discount appears to reflect more of a wait‑and‑see stance by the market, as investors assess the company’s ability to execute in a new, highly competitive environment where it has yet to build a proven operating track record.

On the other hand, the pattern is broadly similar when compared to its direct competitors in Vaca Muerta. GPRK's deeply discounted valuation is paired with very depressed growth metrics, both versus the industry and historical terms, which may suggest the market is still pricing in a high execution risk.

By contrast, this does not appear to be the case for other operators in the basin, whose shares are trading at higher multiples despite robust production growth over the past year.

Risks

Colombia remains by far GPRK's largest market (96% of revenue in Q3). Yet competition for high-quality blocks is intense. State-owned Ecopetrol produces ~60% of the country's hydrocarbons and controls most of the transportation and refining capacity, forcing smaller producers, such as GPRK, to accept less favorable commercial terms or look abroad for growth. At the same time, the overall operating environment for upstream oil in Colombia has severely deteriorated under the Petro administration. Anti-hydrocarbon policies, such as the suspension of new blocks bidding, regulatory changes, and higher taxes, have made Colombia an increasingly hostile jurisdiction.

With that in mind, while it is positive GPRK's entry into Vaca Muerta, the most productive blocks in the Neuquen Basin remain in the hands of well-established operators such as YPF, Pampa (PAM), Vista, or Tecpetrol, with significantly greater drilling capacity. While YPF had completed approximately 159 wells and Vista roughly 60 by the end of Q3, GPRK had completed just 14 wells across all its projects over the same period. This underscores the significant scale gap the company still needs to close to be competitive and to reach its target of drilling 50-55 wells in Vaca Muerta, which significantly increases execution risks and financing requirements, in my view.

Conclusion

To finalize, I maintain a constructive view on GPRK stock, expecting it to break through the current resistance around $10 and move towards the $12-$15 range. That said, such upside will require concrete progress in Vaca Muerta developments over the next year. In the meantime, GPRK's valuation, currently trading at significant discounts versus peers, may benefit in the near term from the positive momentum in Argentine energy stocks.

data.title: RING: A Simple And Effective Way To Capture Gold's Stronger 2026 Outlook
data.description:
Every time I look back at the evolution of gold, I have the same feeling, and it is that this market no longer looks like last year, and I do not say it just to see it above $4000/oz, but because of how the metal is moving.

I do not feel that it is just a short-lived move, but I feel that gold has found another floor. And we know that when such a sensitive asset starts to stabilize at a new level, the whole sector ends up adjusting around that price.

With iShares MSCI Global Gold Miners ETF (RING), that’s exactly what happens to me. When I review it, I see an ETF that accompanied this change without relying on a one-off catalyst, without shocks, and with a consistency that I personally value very much in mining.

In July, when I made my first coverage, RING was close to $43.5. Since then it has advanced 62.4%, well above the 9.2% of the S&P 500. In September I updated it again, and from there, it added another 14.4%, while the global index rose barely 2.3%. This path confirms something I already sensed, and that is that RING not only followed the gold but also managed to translate that cycle straight into price.

With this context, and the feeling that gold can still hold or even expand this new range in 2026, I maintain exactly the same stance as in my two previous coverages: Strong Buy.

The gold market today: it is not an impulse, it is a new range

In recent weeks I have seen several signs that reinforce this impression, and I would like to mention two articles published in the last few days that go in exactly the same direction. The first captures the vision of Deutsche Bank, which projects a range that could approach $5,000/oz in 2026, supported by central bank demand that remains very strong and a market that is tighter than it seems at first glance. The second comes from UBS, which sets a bullish case of $4,900/oz in a scenario with political noise, global uncertainty, and an increasing probability of rate cuts in the United States.

If we add to that the current gold price behavior, holding above $4,200/oz with a stability that we did not see in previous cycles, I think it’s easier to see why those projections don’t look unrealistic.

RING and its structure: concentration, yes, but in names that best translate the price

One of the things I like most about RING is that it does not spread its exposure, but focuses on companies that really convert price into margins. Some companies I’ve looked at previously, such as Newmont (NEM), Agnico Eagle (AEM) and Barrick (B), sit inside a top-ten block that makes up about 67% of the ETF, and I think that this concentration in mature operators is part of what explains their more stable behaviour.

Something that I also think is important is how the fund’s numbers have accompanied these months. RING not only followed the trend of gold, but it did so with a speed that is noticeable, as it has accumulated more than 130% return in the last twelve months, well above what is usually seen across the average of ETFs in the market. For me this confirms that the fund captured the change in metal level almost in real time.

We also have to add liquidity that allows us to operate without shocks and about $2.74B in assets under management, which strengthen the structure even in times of volatility. Although I consider that it is not a cheap ETF, it is efficient, and I believe that in such a volatile universe as mining, that efficiency ends up being worth more than one tenth of a fee.

The dividend is low, as usually happens in companies that prioritize reinvestment, but the financial discipline of recent years shows that the structure is solid. Basically, this is an ETF designed to accompany the gold cycle, not to offer dividend yield.

Macro reading: what the Fed says matters more than it seems

I can’t talk about gold without looking at the United States, because the metal moves at the pace of macro data and how the Fed reads every signal in the cycle. In recent weeks I have heard several comments from officials that, at least for me, help explain part of the recent rise.

Each said it in slightly different ways, but the message goes in the same direction: a rate cut that could come sooner than expected. And if 2026 moves toward a more flexible monetary policy, gold would have even more room to continue rising, as we read in the estimates from Deutsche Bank and UBS. In scenarios like this, I see that mining ETFs capture that movement almost immediately.

Risks: they don’t disappear, but today’s context softens several

While so far everything looks pretty good, I always keep in mind that the risks don’t go away. The first, and for me the most obvious, is gold’s dependence on what the Fed does. A change in tone or in the pace of cuts could cool the momentum faster than many anticipate.

Something related to this is the global climate of uncertainty, which today supports much of the demand for safe-haven assets. If that noise fades, it seems logical to me that gold could ease a bit, because part of the current demand comes from this global tension.

There are also the risks inherent to the mining sector: costs that may move more than expected, labor tensions, operational delays, or complex jurisdictions. Although I think that in RING they tend to dilute because there are several companies and geographies, they are still factors to watch closely.

Conclusion

After looking at the whole picture, from the price of gold to the behavior of the ETF, I still see RING exactly the same way I did in my two previous updates. I get the feeling gold has settled into a new, higher, and more stable range, and the ETF not only followed that shift, but translated it into price with a consistency that is unusual in this sector.

With a solid structure, a concentration that works in its favor, and a context that could still give gold more room in 2026, nothing in the thesis weakened. If anything, it feels stronger, which is why I keep the same stance as before: Strong Buy.

data.title: To Meet Your Investment Goals, Focus On Yields Rather Than Spreads
data.description:
Spreads are essential for investors who utilize leverage, but for most fixed income investors, yields are much more important. Fortunately, in today’s market, yields are relatively attractive despite historically tight spreads.

Yields vs. Spreads: Different Information for Different Investors

Yields and spreads are two common metrics used to evaluate fixed income securities, but they serve different purposes for different types of investors.

In our view, yields are far more relevant for absolute return investors. The yield on a bond is the return an investor can expect to receive at maturity, assuming the bond does not default. Thus, for traditional, long-only investors (i.e., those that do not use borrowed funds to purchase bonds), yields are what matter.

Whether the investor is an endowment that needs to fund its operations or an individual trying to meet a savings goal (college tuition, home ownership, retirement, etc.), the best way to estimate future returns is to look at yields, not spreads.

In contrast, spread is the difference in yield between a fixed income asset and a reference rate (typically a U.S. Treasury bond of the same maturity). Investors demand excess compensation when investing in non-Treasury bonds to offset real and perceived risks, including liquidity, collateral, prepayment, default, and recovery (among others).

For fixed income investors that rely on leverage (i.e., buy bonds with borrowed money), such as hedge funds, spreads are essential. Their funding is always pegged to a reference rate, and their entire business depends on earning more than they are paying to borrow.

Said differently, these investors are laser-focused on the difference between their cost of funds and their returns, which is most easily measured in spread. Similarly, Wall Street trading desks price the securities they hold relative to the risk-free rate, given that they also have funding costs, so the entire ecosystem of levered investors and market makers relies on spreads as a proxy for price.1

Yields Can Be Attractive Even If Spreads Are Tight

In theory, spreads are a great market signal, as they tend to widen when risk is elevated and tighten when times are good. But in practice, they can rise and fall for many other reasons, which limits their value as an information source. For example, spreads can move simply because the underlying reference rate changes, which has nothing to do with the credit quality of the issuer. As a result, yields can be attractive whether spreads are wide or tight, and vice versa.

The current environment underscores this point. As we can see below, the spread of the ICE BofA High Yield Index is extremely tight right now – near the low point of the last 20 years.

Yields, however, tell a different story. Despite historically tight spreads, they are fairly attractive right now, and they are about 250 basis points above the lows in 2021.

Similarly, index-level spreads can oversimplify what is happening in the market. For example, the current spread level of the ICE BofA High Yield Index is near its all-time low, suggesting that credit risk is also near a low point. But when we look at the index broken out by rating, we see a much different picture.

Spreads are in fact quite tight for BBs and BBBs, the two highest rating categories, but for CCCs they are substantially wider, reflecting the drop-off in credit quality. As the table below shows, CCC spreads are in the 73rd percentile over the past 20 years, compared to the 95th and 100th for BBs and Bs, respectively.

Historical Spread Comparisons Are Challenging

Another limitation of index-level spreads is that they are difficult to compare across longer time horizons, as index compositions can change. For example, as we have discussed previously, the quality of the high-yield bond market has improved substantially since the Great Financial Crisis, and the ICE BofA High Yield Index looks very different than it did in 2008.

Today it is much more heavily weighted in the highest rating category (BB), while the lowest rating categories combined (CCC and below) have been nearly cut in half.

This re-weighting needs to be considered when comparing the spread of the index over time, as it is not an apples-to-apples comparison. As you can see in the table below, the high-yield index spread is currently at 304 basis points, which makes it appear close to the 2007 level of 253 basis points.

However, if we were to take the weightings of the index from 2007 and apply 2025 spreads (by rating category), it would equate to an index spread of 359 basis points today. Conversely, if we were to apply today’s weightings to 2007, the spread would have been 231 basis points.

Final Thoughts

Where does this leave us? At the end of the third quarter, yields for the high yield index were 7.1%, which was the 40th percentile for the past 20 years. As value investors, we would always prefer to buy quality companies at higher yields, but from an absolute return perspective, these levels are still creating good opportunities for the portfolio and are not flashing signs of a bubble.

In addition, between 1995 and 2025, the average 1-year forward return in periods that started with a 7% or higher yield is much better than the average for periods that started with a sub-7% yield.2

Still, we caution against making investment decisions based on a single factor. In our view, flexible, actively managed portfolios that consider the economic and market cycle work better than strategies with rigid, preset triggers.

1 Interestingly, spreads begin to lose their efficacy when a company is in distress. In these situations, the issuer’s bonds (and credit default swaps) move from trading on spread to trading on price. In other words, investors begin focusing on the borrower’s probability of default, and the underlying risk-free rate plays no role in determining the market price.

2 This statement is based on monthly data for the ICE BofA High Yield Index for the period 12/31/1994 through 10/31/2025. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

These risks may increase for emerging markets. Investments in debt securities typically decrease in value when interest rates rise. This risk is usually greater for longer-term debt securities. Small- and mid-capitalization companies tend to have limited liquidity and greater price volatility than large-capitalization companies. Higher turnover rates may result in increased transaction costs, which could impact performance. data.title: 50 Largest U.S. Banks By Total Assets, Q3 2025
data.description:
M&A activity led to some reshuffling of the US banking industry asset rankings as large banks showcased greater confidence in dealmaking.

The 50 largest US banks reported a $282.98 billion increase in aggregate assets to a combined $25.432 trillion during the third quarter, with 35 institutions recording asset growth, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data. However, the growth was slower than a year ago when an increase in assets from the three biggest banks — JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Bank of America Corp. (BAC) and Citigroup Inc. (C) — helped push the top 50's sequential aggregate asset growth to $377.22 billion in the third quarter of 2024.

Big-ticket transactions

On Oct. 6, Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) announced its $10.85 billion bid to acquire Comerica Inc. (CMA), making it the largest US bank deal announced since 2021 and the first bank transaction with a deal value exceeding $10 billion since the same year. The Cincinnati-based bank's assets were adjusted higher by $77.51 billion to reflect its pending acquisition, representing 38.3% sequential growth. Fifth Third ranked as the 16th-largest US bank by total assets in the third quarter, up from 23rd in the prior quarter.

Meanwhile, Huntington Bancshares Inc.'s (HBAN) assets were adjusted higher by $66.04 billion to reflect its $1.87 billion acquisition of Veritex Holdings Inc., which closed Oct. 20, and its pending $7.59 billion acquisition of Cadence Bank (CADE), announced Oct. 27. Huntington's purchases of Cadence and Veritex were the third- and sixth-largest US bank deals announced in 2025, respectively.

Huntington's total assets grew sequentially by 25.4%, the second highest among the nation's 50 largest banks. Huntington moved two notches higher from its previous ranking, clinching the 18th spot in the third quarter.

PNC Financial Services Group Inc. (PNC) recorded the third-highest sequential increase in total assets, with 6.5% growth in the third quarter. PNC's assets were adjusted higher by $26.70 billion to reflect its bid to acquire FirstBank Holding Co. for $4.04 billion, which was the fourth-largest bank deal announced in 2025.

Big 4 banks' combined assets increase

Aggregate assets at the four largest US banks grew sequentially by $71.71 billion, or 0.6%, in the third quarter, compared to 3.0% growth in the previous quarter.

JPMorgan, the biggest US bank at $4.560 trillion in total assets as of Sept. 30, reported a quarter-over-quarter increase of $7.72 billion in assets, or 0.2%, in the third quarter.

Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) posted the largest increase in total assets among the Big Four banks, with 4.1% sequential growth, or an increase of $81.71 billion in assets.

Citigroup reported asset growth of 0.8% from the prior quarter, while Bank of America posted a sequential decline of 1.1%, or $37.43 billion, in assets.

The Big Four logged robust results in the third quarter with growth in spread and fee income, as well as a decline in credit costs.

Growth outlook

According to Stephens analysts, the recent spurt of regional bank M&A indicates increased large M&A activity heading into year-end and 2026. As deal activity unfolds, the analysts picked First Horizon Corp., Webster Financial Corp., Flagstar Bank NA (FLG) and Wintrust Financial Corp. (WTFC) as likely candidates for future acquisitions.

The resurgence of bank M&A activity in 2025 was also a catalyst for recent balance sheet restructurings. According to Piper Sandler President Deb Schoneman, engagement with depository clients over balance sheet repositioning has increased as interest rates decline and the yield curve deepens.

About this analysis

To conduct this analysis, S&P Global Market Intelligence examined the largest US banks and thrifts by assets with a deposits-to-assets ratio of at least 25% or at least $30 billion in deposits as of quarter-end.

To compile a pro forma ranking, S&P Global Market Intelligence calculates pro forma assets after accounting for pending M&A transactions as well as transactions that closed after quarter-end. To be included in pro forma adjustments, the deal value must be over $1 billion or involve assets or deposits in excess of $5 billion. Loan portfolio deals are not included because of a general lack of data on both deal consideration and the impact on total assets.
