data.title: TORM: The Bottom Of The Cycle Isn't In Yet
data.description:
Introduction

Over the last few years, the product tanker industry has had a major pullback, and with it being a cyclical industry, it caught my attention as we might be closer to a bottom in the cycle. In particular, TORM (TRMD) is an interesting company because it's a major pure play on product tanker rates.

With cyclical industries like product tankers, stocks in the sector can become very overvalued or undervalued easily, which provides opportunities for investors to tactically get in at the right time.

With this said, though, I think there are still some bearish supply-side catalysts in the short to medium term; hence, I don't think the bottom for voyage charter rates is in yet.

In this write-up, I'll go over:

  • Understanding how skewness in TORM impacts returns
  • Orderbook
  • Red Sea impact
  • Refinery closure impact
  • Return expectations for bear, base, and bull environments & how to work that into a valuation

Return Distribution

To understand how to approach TORM, I wanted to go over the distribution of returns and how that impacts long-term total returns.

Equities generally have a left tail to their return distribution, with frequent small up years and less frequent sharp down years. The product and crude tanker industry is the opposite, with most years having low returns and a few small periods of time making up for a bulk of the returns. This can be seen when looking at the Baltic Clean Tanker Index:

This creates a right-tailed skew. I would visualise it as the distribution below:

This has an impact on total returns and the way investors should approach a company like TORM.

What happens with a right-tail skew, as TORM has, is that most dividends will come in up years, when the stock price is high, and fewer dividends will come in down years, when the stock price is lower. If you reinvest dividends (the total return assumes you do), this means you're reinvesting your dividends into a higher stock price.

This is the opposite of how it works for most stocks that have a left-tailed distribution. For instance, say you have a stock at $100 with a fixed dividend of $10; now, when the stock price goes to $50 with the same fixed dividend, you're able to buy twice as many shares, and vice versa if the stock price were to increase. Effectively, you are reinvesting more into a lower stock price and less into a higher one. You'd expect a return of 10% (assuming no stock price appreciation or depreciation), but in actuality, your CAGR would be higher depending on the level of variance because you're heavily buying the dips and buying less when it rips. In other words, you're doing a form of volatility harvesting.

The above example is a bit of an overstatement since if a stock falls 50%, its dividend will likely get cut too, although not likely as much as the stock price fell (past recessions show this is the case). Nonetheless, the broader point is that you are buying at a lower valuation, as was the case during the GFC when the Shiller PE fell 50%.

This isn't the case for TORM. Below is a chart of dividends per share, per quarter:

When the stock price was at its 5-year low of around $7 in 2021, no dividends were paid. At its peak for two quarters, the dividend was $7.01 in 2023, and the stock price was around $26. Now the last quarter's dividend was $2.02, which is a decrease of 71%, all while the stock decreased 16.5% from then. Simply put, the dividend yield decreased considerably as the stock price decreased: the opposite of most stocks. As a result, dividend reinvestment works against investors: you end up buying more shares at the peak (when payouts are high) and fewer shares near the lows (when payouts are small), which drags on long-term total returns.

In effect, TORM looks cheap when it’s actually expensive and looks expensive when it’s actually cheap because the dividend yield and valuation more broadly are highly pro-cyclical rather than counter-cyclical.

I lead with this because if you misunderstand the cycle, you misunderstand TORM. Its payout structure flips the typical volatility harvesting dynamic, making passive holding less effective; instead, active mean-reversion positioning is far more effective. I believe this context matters because it shapes how TORM should be approached.

Orderbook

The biggest reason I still think we have lower to go in rates is the large order book in product tankers:

Source

As seen above, we're at a nine-year high in terms of the percentage of the current fleet. But this chart doesn't show it broken down by the exact segment. TORM's is made up of primarily LR1, LR2, and MR. Because in recent history (2022-2024), rates were very high with an aging fleet for these particular product tankers, the order book for them has increased even more than for other segments, currently sitting at 40%.

Aframax/LR2 tonnage has led the surge in new orders. AXSMarine data indicate that the Aframax/LR2 orderbook jumped from around 11% to near 40% of that fleet's capacity within just a couple of years, reflecting the segment's popularity among both established owners and newer entrants. Over 200 Aframax/LR2 newbuilds, accounting for more than 23m MT of deadweight, are on order and scheduled for delivery over 2025–2027.

40% of the segment being brought on as new inventory would drastically reduce rates, all else being equal. Not to mention that the scrapping of old vessels won't be enough to offset this. According to Lloyd's List, only 17.5% of the fleet is 21 years or older.

We also have to remember that pricing is inelastic, so just a small increase in supply will bring significant downside to spot rates and utilisation rates.

Red Sea and Refinery Closures

Revenue has been high due to Red Sea and refinery disruptions. There are two ways of viewing this. Firstly, many are viewing this as bullish, saying it will keep revenue higher. But the counterpoint to this is that the market has already priced in high revenue expectations, and that enough supply will come on the market to meet this demand. I'm squarely in the latter camp.

Red Sea and refinery closure reroutings:

Source

When it comes to using the Suez or rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope, the decision is financial and operational.

On the financial side, it comes down to the cost of insurance. Pre-crisis, the insurance premiums via the Suez Canal were minimal, so it was a no-brainer to use that route. Now, though, it's gotten to the point where the extra premium for war insurance is so much higher than the extra cost of going around the Cape of Good Hope:

War risk insurance is gaining popularity on solid ground, as well, especially from companies operating in high-risk areas. Policy costs are typically kept quiet, but one war risk insurance underwriter told the BBC that premiums for a British or American company operating in Lebanon or Israel are currently priced at between 0.5 percent and 2 percent of the total cover they buy. So, if a business wants $100 million of annual cover, it would pay between $500,000 and $2 million in war risk insurance premiums. The site’s regional or national volatility or stability would change the rate—premiums in stable Gulf states are much lower, estimated at between 0.025 to 0.05 percent.

So as long as insurance is priced such that it's uneconomic to use the Suez, that route will likely not come back anytime soon.

The second issue with the Suez is operational. Even if it becomes cheaper to use the Suez, it's not a simple flip of the switch. Essentially, a network structure has now been built for the Cape of Good Hope, and it would be a multi-month process to move it back to the Suez.

So a move back is a real long-term commitment that most companies don't want to make right now.

As for the refineries' closure increasing demand, what's been happening is that major California refineries, such as those owned by Valero and Phillips 66, are closing down.

Much of the existing refinery infrastructure is aging, and increasing regulations make it more costly to operate.

California requires CARBOB (California Reformulated Blendstock for Oxygenate Blending), which is a specific type of blendstock that refineries have to make to meet stricter environmental standards.

The EIA commented on California's stricter environmental standards:

Refinery crack spreads on the West Coast are typically higher than they are in other parts of the country, in part because of tighter supply-demand balances in the region, reflected by the lower average days of supply. Refiners in California must comply with the state’s Cap-and-Trade program, which requires them to bid for emissions allowances, as well as the state’s LCFS, which requires refiners (and importers or wholesalers) to buy carbon credits according to the volume of carbon-emitting fuels (such as gasoline and petroleum diesel) that they supply to the market. The costs of compliance with these measures are partly reflected in the wider wholesale crack spreads. As a result, higher crack spreads alone may not necessarily indicate higher net profits for refiners in the state, although they do indicate relatively higher prices for gasoline overall. The West Coast also tends to have higher refiner acquisition costs for crude oil because crude oil production is limited in the region and routes to import crude oil are often longer. The higher acquisition costs do not directly affect the crack spread for gasoline, but they do contribute to higher overall fuel prices.

Put simply, the crack spread in California is far wider, but refineries aren't getting paid extra for that wider spread. The extra spread goes into meeting the more stringent requirements set by the state.

So California's demand has turned to shipping in finished products since they don't refine enough locally and don't have enough pipeline infrastructure to meet the demand:

According to energy shipping specialists Poten & Partners, the refinery shutdown will increase the amount of fuel that California imports from East Asian refiners. Domestic tanker capacity is nearly at full utilization, and there is no orderbook for additional U.S.-built tankers at present, so Poten believes that additional shipments from the Gulf Coast to California are unlikely. Instead, the consultancy suggests, the replacement fuel will likely come from the place with the greatest capacity to produce it at a favorable cost - East Asia.

"We will likely see more imports from longhaul suppliers in Asia to satisfy product demand from California. South Korea is by far California’s largest supplier, but Singapore and India are also regular exporters to the U.S. West Coast. China is another potential supplier of refined products to California," noted Poten. "By the end of 2025 and into 2026, these developments could boost product tanker employment in the Pacific Basin."

So, at least for the time being, this increases demand for product tankers. And with the strict regulatory requirements of California, it's unlikely in the near term that a new pipeline (connecting to less-regulated states) or refinery will be approved.

For these reasons, I expect that Cape of Good Hope and California product tanker demands will stay elevated.

Although what I'm saying might sound bullish, I don't view it as such. In the commodity markets, the timeless adage is that the cure for high prices is high prices. The Red Sea disruptions by the Houthis have already been happening for over a year, and with other supply issues happening prior, such as the COVID-era refinery shutdown & maintenance and the war in Ukraine (sanctions on Russian-originated oil), product tanker demand has been high for many years. Similar story for refineries, as it wasn't a shock when they announced closures, since increased regulations have long been an issue in California.

So for these reasons, my view is that the market has known about these areas of increased demand and already looked to offset it with the large order book. And if these areas of demand become 'the new normal', then order book demand will continue to increase. So new supply will come on the market to meet the new demand. Put another way, I think all these bullish structural issues are priced in.

Returns Expectations & Valuation

I want to go over return expectations for a bullish and bearish rate environment. These return expectations can be used as a way to work into what a fair valuation would be.

One of the nice things about TORM is that they report TCE (Time Charter Equivalent). TCE is (voyage revenue - voyage expenses)/days available.

This makes things easier for us since voyage expenses are a cost of revenue that is variable, hence hard to calculate. Similarly, the number of days available (utilisation rate) is also difficult to estimate. So we can think of TCE as a proxy for gross profit, with just fixed expenses, such as operating expenses and D&A, needing to be subtracted to get operating income.

Sticking with the last quarter as the base case, there's $99 million in operating income, annualised to $396 million. At the time of writing, the market cap is $2.21 billion, with cash of $209.3 million and debt of $899.4 million ($121.9 million as the current portion of LT debt + $767 million as long-term debt + $10.5 million as capital leases), making for an enterprise value of $2.9 billion.

The reason I use operating income is that I want to focus on the operating drivers of the business, hence excluding interest and taxes. Similarly, I use EV to look at the business, inclusive of its capital structure.

Current EBIT/EV is 13.66%.

To get an idea of what rates in a bearish scenario look like, we can compare current rates to the lows in 2017 & 2018. Both LR2 and MR were around half of what they are right now, as seen above.

Voyage costs, which are the cost of revenue, such as fuel costs, port charges, broker fees, and hedging costs, are difficult to quantify in a low-rate environment. If rates decrease due to an economic contraction, then it's likely that these voyage costs will also decrease. But if instead we see a low-rate environment caused by an oversupply of vessels but steady demand, these voyage costs are likely to remain the same. For this reason, we can calculate scenarios for both.

Supply glut scenario: the volume of vessels increases, but demand stays stable, and most importantly, voyage cost inputs are stable because demand hasn't been reduced.

Last quarter's TCE was $236 million with a top line of $342.6 million. So that's a voyage expense of $106.6 million (difference between TCE & revenue). Now, if the revenue is down 50%, it would be $171.3 million, which makes for $64.7 million in expected TCE if keeping voyage expenses the same.

Fixed cash expenses, the difference between TCE and EBITDA, are $84 million, and D&A is $53 million. This makes for a quarterly EBITDA of -$19.3 million and EBIT of -$72.3 million. Annualising this, the EBITDA is -$77.2 million, and EBIT is -$289.2 million.

In this scenario, EBIT/EV is -9.93%.

Similarly, we can calculate a bearish rate scenario due to a demand drop. I would make the same assumptions as above, but cut voyage costs by 20%, as it's likely that during an economic contraction, we see deflationary pressures on certain input costs such as fuel. A 20% reduction in voyage cost makes for $85.28 million. Sticking with the same revenue of $171.3 million, that's a TCE of $86.02 million. Removing the same fixed cash expenses, we have an EBITDA of $2.02 million. Removing non-cash expenses, D&A, we get -$50.98 million in EBIT. Annualising this, the EBITDA is $8.08 million, and EBIT is -$203.92 million.

This makes for an EBIT/EV of -7.03%.

We can then calculate a bullish scenario. There are two ways a bullish scenario would play out. One is a shortage of supply, which causes a structural bull market. I find this highly unlikely due to the already large order book. The second is a demand shock from geopolitical or similar events. This is far more plausible. For this reason, I'll only entertain the demand shock.

In this scenario, let's say rates go up 50%. And voyage expenses increase 20%, as input costs like fuel would increase in this scenario. Doubling last quarter's revenue, it's $685.2 million, and a 20% increase in voyage cost is $127.92 million, making for a TCE of $557.28 million. The same fixed cash expenses would make for an EBITDA of $473.28 million. And an EBIT, or operating income, of $420.28 million. Annualised, that's $1681.12 million. As a percentage of EV, that's 57.95%.

To recap:

Base case; current EBIT: $396 million; EBIT/EV: 13.66%

Bearish EBIT scenario due to supply glut: -$289.2 million; EBIT/EV: -9.93%

Bearish EBIT scenario due to demand drop: -$203.92 million; EBIT/EV: -7.03%

Bullish EBIT scenario due to demand spike: $1681.12 million; EBIT/EV: 57.95%

We can use these expected scenarios to work into a valuation.

Say we assign the following probabilities:

Base case: 55%

Bearish supply glut: 25%

Bearish demand crash: 10%

Bullish demand spike: 10%

Expected return: (0.55*13.66%) + (0.25*-9.93%) + (0.1*-7.03%) + (0.1*57.95%) = 10.1225%

I would call a 10.1225% expected return here slightly overvalued. Essentially, when looking at expected return, I would compare it to a hurdle rate, and for a highly cyclical company like TORM with high operating leverage, most investors would have a higher hurdle rate. So I consider a 12% - 15% hurdle rate as fair.

Now, there are a lot of caveats and potential flaws to this analysis. Firstly, I used the worst case for all scenarios except the base case. In reality, there will be time spent in the middle, but the idea here is that it evens itself out. I also excluded a bullish supply-based scenario since I don't see that happening with the large order book, instead opting to increase the base case percentage. The other assumption is nonlinear cost behaviour. I assumed that in a demand spike or crash, we would see voyage costs change alongside it, but there's no guarantee we will. The last major caveat is that I used last quarter's figures and then annualised them, but many would prefer full-year figures to avoid seasonality issues. Although this is true, the effects of seasonality are very low compared to the extremes in these scenarios, so the impact isn't much. I also didn't change utilisation rates.

That said, even with the caveats and potential flaws, I still think this is a good scenario analysis framework to use. If you disagree with my assumptions, you can plug and play with your own and compare the result to what your hurdle rate is.

Investor Takeaways

When putting all the moving parts together, the current setup for TORM looks more mid-cycle than cycle trough here. Yes, there's continued structural demand from the Red Sea rerouting and refinery closures, but supply will come on the market to meet that demand.

As I mentioned at the beginning, the cycle matters here the most due to the return distribution. Because TORM is in an industry where the entry point, rather than steady compounding, does the heavy lifting. When you buy into high earnings and tight markets, forward returns tend to cool off. When you buy after rates and sentiment reset, the payoff is much better. Right now, we’re much closer to the former than the latter.

Bottom line: this isn't a call that TORM is a bad long position here, but I don't see any asymmetry to the upside, nor does the valuation method used show it's undervalued or has a margin of safety. For these reasons, it's not the right part of the cycle to expect outsized returns. The better opportunity likely shows up after the rate pressure from newbuild deliveries plays out and the market gets less enthusiastic about current earnings.

Therefore, I have put a hold rating on TORM.

data.title: From Black Friday To Fed Wednesday: The Year-End Data Investors Must Watch
data.description:
Plenty of official economic data is already coming down the market’s chimney, much to the delight of our partners at Econoday. Now that the pesky government shutdown is over, the pace of macro updates is set to accelerate as we head into the end of 2025.

We already received key November purchasing managers' index (PMI) readings from S&P Global and the Institute for Supply Management (ISM) earlier this week. Then, this morning’s ADP private-sector employment report shed new light on the health of that swath of the labor market.

A Frosty Look at the Jobs Environment

Thursday and Friday could be particularly revealing regarding employment. According to our Economic Calendar, the first thing Thursday morning (December 4) comes is the in-vogue Challenger Job Cuts report. Recall that for October, the aggregate number of announced layoff intentions soared to the most since 2003 for that month. Of course, Challenger’s monthly tallies were higher this past February and March, when federal government employment took a hit courtesy of the Department of Government Efficiency’s efforts.

Thursday also provides a timely glimpse into the trend of applications for unemployment benefits. Take this week’s Initial and Continuing Claims data with a grain of salt, though, as there’s notorious trouble with properly accounting for holiday-week dynamics. Nevertheless, Initial Claims remain sanguine, while Continuing Claims hover near the highest level in four years. “Slow to hire, slow to fire”—no changes on that macro front.

Leftovers on the Economic Table

This week’s data twist plays out on Friday (December 5). Stale data hits the tape at 10 a.m. ET by way of September Personal Income and Spending numbers. The Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation barometer—the Personal Consumption Expenditure (PCE) Price Index—is often the market-moving detail.

Maybe not so much this time. Bond traders are nearly locked in on a quarter-point rate cut next Wednesday afternoon, so even a hot report probably won’t sway the Fed. What’s more, being a September flash, it’s about as fresh as last week’s turkey and pumpkin pie at this point.

Checking the Consumer’s Holiday Spirit

Potentially more interesting will be the first read of December consumer sentiment on Friday (December 5). The University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers is no longer as much of a volatility catalyst as it once was, but in light of robust retail activity leading into and after Black Friday, we’ll see if vibes have turned merrier.

Also affecting households’ financial feelings may be the resumption of government assistance programs (on the lower end) and a stock market recovery after recent volatility (on the higher end). And we can’t talk about the consumer without a nod to the K-shaped economy, which is the theme of the holiday shopping season.

Inflation Under Wraps

Peeking into next week, the New York Fed’s Consumer Inflation Expectations survey prints at 11 a.m. ET on Monday, December 8. Individuals and business owners have come to expect elevated consumer price increases—near 3% annually across timeframes—but there have been interesting developments in traded inflation markets.

Interest-rate swaps on the year-ahead inflation rate suggest a slower rise in the Consumer Price Index (CPI). If the markets are correct, a 2.6% CPI annual pace will be seen by this time in 2026. Above the Fed’s 2% target? Yes. But it would be an improvement from today’s 3% level (as of September).

It’s little solace for embattled American entrepreneurs, however. Tuesday’s (December 9) main macro events include the October Job Openings and Labor Turnover (JOLTS) survey (the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics canceled the September report), a final update to Q3 Nonfarm Productivity, and the National Federation of Independent Business (NFIB) Small Business Optimism Index.

The NFIB report contains clues and nuggets about the employment situation, inflation, and broader economic perceptions among the growth drivers of the American workforce. A positive turn in this “soft” data could lead to improved “hard” data later.

Gather Round the Fed’s Fireplace

All of it leads into the Fed’s Wednesday (December 10) afternoon interest rate decision. A third consecutive quarter-point cut is expected, but that might not be the most important thing. More dramatic may be the makeup of the 12 votes.

Assuming a 25-basis point cut, there could be at least a few hawkish dissenters. Chair Powell has done an effective job of herding the cats, so to speak, throughout his tenure as Fed chief, but there are now outspoken doves and hawks who are not afraid to buck the consensus.

Tidings From the Dot Plot and Some Missing Data Under the Macro Tree

Powell’s press conference begins at 2:30 p.m. ET on Wednesday, and he may have his work cut out for himself to form a cohesive logic for cutting in the face of stubborn inflation. The media Q&A could be muddled, but investors have other details to parse—these come via the quarterly Summary of Economic Projections (SEP), otherwise known as the dot plot. The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) jots down its latest forecasts for GDP growth, inflation, and unemployment.

The voting members may be better served by simply waiting eight days to decide the next step along their monetary policy path. Why? The fresh November Employment Situation report hits the following Tuesday morning (December 16), while November CPI is released on Thursday, December 18. Alas, there are no plans to push back the December Fed gathering. So, before Christmas, we should have a decent bead on the Fed’s January move.

Absent from the Economic Calendar is the Retail Sales report. The U.S. Census Bureau has not announced when it plans to publish November numbers.

The Bottom Line

As the holiday season wraps up, the economic data stream keeps markets busy. Job reports, consumer sentiment, and inflation readings will give investors plenty to unwrap in the coming days. Along with comments from CEOs at conferences this week and next, the upcoming data deluge and Fed decision will shape how 2025 finishes.
